evidence_quotes.quote: This is, in my opinion, a form of dark pattern - they use high initial costs, market the game like physical products but deliver it as a service to maximize profit through microtransactions.
When I buy a video game, I expect to be able to play it for as long as I want.
they deliver games as a license, where they can choose to terminate it at any time even though consumers still want to play the game.

paragraphs.text: Hello, I want to begin by saying that I am proud to be an EU citizen. The EU continues to prove itself as being pro-consumer and a great example for the rest of the world. With that said, I would like to suggest that you include two related problems that affect digital justice for millions of Europeans. The first problem concerns what happens to video games we have purchased when companies decide to shut them down. The Stop Destroying Games initiative has received over 1.4 million signatures, which shows the scope of this issue. When I buy a video game, I expect to be able to play it for as long as I want. But companies can shut down the game permanently whenever it suits them, and then all access is lost for everyone. As consumers, we have the right to get what we pay for. Millions of people lose games they have paid for, and we cannot show our future children classics the same way we got to experience Super Mario. A physical book or DVD I can keep for as long as I want, but digital games can be taken away from me at any time despite being marketed and sold in the same way. This undermines our fundamental consumer rights. Game companies sell their games for large sums of money and then offer subscription services and in-game purchases in most cases. Despite this pricing, they deliver games as a license, where they can choose to terminate it at any time even though consumers still want to play the game. This is, in my opinion, a form of dark pattern - they use high initial costs, market the game like physical products but deliver it as a service to maximize profit through microtransactions. When the servers are shut down, they face no consequences and keep all the money from us consumers. The second problem concerns payment processors growing control over what consumers can buy online. Recently, payment processors like Visa, Mastercard, and PayPal threatened to terminate their services to gaming platforms like Steam and Itch.io if they did not remove certain legal adult and horror games. This happened after pressure from activist groups who could not influence the platforms directly. When just a few payment processors can decide which legal products we can buy, a form of private censorship arises that circumvents both national laws and EU regulations. Consumers lose access to legal content, and smaller companies can be completely shut out of the market. Payment processors have become so dominant that they function as a bottleneck for the entire digital economy. This gives them disproportionate power to shape the market according to their own preferences rather than according to law and democratic processes. We deserve the same protection digitally as we have for physical products. You are already working to make the digital market more fair for consumers, and both of these issues would fit well into this work. Consumers should have the right to long-term access to digital products they have purchased, and payment companies should treat all legal transactions neutrally without discriminating based on content that is legal in member countries. Given the enormous support for the Stop Destroying Games initiative and the growing problem of payment processors power, both of these issues seem to be something that really needs attention to ensure true digital justice. Thank you for listening to us consumers and for your important work in creating a more just digital future.
